name: Maternal GFR in first trimester
tier_rationale: Tier A from Davison & Hytten 1974 BJOG: foundational study of GFR across pregnancy. The ~50% rise above non-pregnant baseline (~90 mL/min) is well-replicated across multiple subsequent studies (cited in Cheung & Lafayette 2013 review). Paywall-limited; specific first-trimester table re-verification pending.
notes: First-trimester GFR, ~50% above non-pregnant baseline of ~90. The Davison 1974 paper is the foundational citation; multiple downstream reviews (including Cheung 2013) confirm the magnitude of rise.

name: Maternal glomerular filtration rate at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Cheung & Lafayette 2013 ACKD review: GFR rises ~50% in pregnancy (~135 mL/min at peak). Multi-source review synthesis; foundational primary studies (e.g., Davison & Hytten 1974) are the cited evidence base.
notes: GFR at term; elevated approximately 40-50% above non-pregnant baseline.

name: Maternal plasma creatinine at term
tier_rationale: Tier A from Cheung & Lafayette 2013 ACKD review: GFR increases ~50% in pregnancy, producing a corresponding fall in serum creatinine, urea, and uric acid. The review's Table 1 reports a representative average serum creatinine of 0.5 mg/dL during pregnancy. Tier A reflects the review's synthesis of multiple sources; term-specific values may differ from the pregnancy-averaged figure.
notes: Term serum creatinine. Cheung 2013 Table 1 lists 0.5 mg/dL as the average during pregnancy; the dataset's 0.6 at term is plausible given mid-third-trimester recovery toward non-pregnant baseline, but the precise term-vs-average distinction needs human re-verification.
